sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=57, col=5), MOUSE(row=58, col=35).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 214, level 3.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 159 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 6.1x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 62 actions returned the board to a state already seen 62 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 62 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so clicking box 1:
- Moved T-shape from rows 16-19, cols 16-19 to rows 24-27, cols 8-11
- Changed Left H from gwww to gBBB (turned OFF)
- Left V stayed ggwg (ON)
Then pressing trigger:
- Moved T-shape from rows 24-27, cols 8-11 to rows 16-19, cols 16-19
- Changed Left H from gBBB to gwww (turned ON)
- Left V stayed ggwg (ON)
So clicking box 1 moves the T-shape to bottom-left (rows 24-27, cols 8-11) and turns OFF left H buttons.
Pressing the trigger moves the T-shape from bottom-left to middle-right (rows 16-19, cols 16-19) and turns ON left H buttons.
This is getting confusing. Let me try to understand the pattern better.
Actually, I notice that the T-shape position and button states seem to be linked. When I click a box, the T-shape moves to a specific position and the buttons change. When I press the trigger, the T-shape moves again and the buttons change again.
Let me try to map out the relationship between box selection, T-shape position, and button states.
Current state:
- T-shape at rows 16-19, cols 16-19 (middle-right)
- Box 4 selected
- Left H: ON, Left V: ON, Right H: OFF, Right V: OFF
Let me try clicking all 4 boxes and recording the T-shape position and button states. This will help me understand the mechanics.
Actually, I've been exploring for 159 actions. I need to stop exploring and commit to a hypothesis.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to make the T-shape match the yellow L-shape in the right panel. The yellow L-shape is at rows 11-15, cols 19-23 in the right panel. The right panel starts at col 33, so the L-shape is at cols 33+19=52 to 33+23=56 in the full grid.
But the T-shape is in the left panel, which is cols 6-30. So the T-shape can't directly overlap with the L-shape.
Wait, maybe the goal is to match the RELATIVE position of the T-shape in the left panel to the relative position of the L-shape in the right panel.
The L-shape is at the top-right of the right panel. So the T-shape should be at the top-right of the left panel: rows 8-11, cols 16-19.
Let me try to move the T-shape to rows 8-11, cols 16-19 and see if the level completes.
Currently, T-shape is at rows 16-19, cols 16-19. I need to move it UP to rows 8-11.
Let me click box 2 to move UP.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click box 2 to move T-shape UP
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 15}])

lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"After clicking box 2:")
for r in range(7, 31):
    row_str = lines[r][6:31]
    if 'c' in row_str:
        print(f"T-shape row {r}: positions {[i for i,c in enumerate(row_str) if c=='c']}")
print(f"Left H: {lines[33][6:26]}")
print(f"Left V: {lines[47][6:26]}")
print(f"Right H: {lines[33][33:53]}")
print(f"Right V: {lines[47][33:53]}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
